title: The Nervous System and Forgiveness: What Letting Go Does to Your Biology
excerpt: Explore how forgiveness impacts your nervous system and body, helping you release trauma and regulate emotions for lasting healing.
body:
Introduction: Forgiveness Beyond the Mind

When we think of forgiveness, most of us imagine a mental or emotional decision—choosing to let go of resentment or anger. However, forgiveness is far more than a thought process; it’s a biological event that profoundly influences the nervous system. As trauma researchers and somatic therapists have shown, unresolved hurt is stored in the body and nervous system, keeping us stuck in chronic stress and dysregulation. Forgiveness, then, can be an essential tool for nervous system regulation and trauma recovery.

In this article, we’ll explore what happens inside your nervous system when you forgive, why it’s not just about ‘feeling better,’ and how embracing forgiveness can be a powerful step towards somatic healing.

Understanding the Nervous System’s Role in Holding Grudges

Stephen Porges, the father of Polyvagal Theory, revolutionized our understanding of the nervous system’s role in social engagement and threat response. When you hold onto anger, resentment, or bitterness, your autonomic nervous system often stays locked in a state of heightened vigilance or shutdown, impacting your health and well-being.

The autonomic nervous system has two main branches:

  • Sympathetic: Activates fight-or-flight responses.
  • Parasympathetic: Supports rest, digestion, and social engagement.

When you carry unresolved hurt, your sympathetic system may be chronically activated, pumping stress hormones like cortisol and adrenaline. This keeps your body on edge, ready to defend, which can manifest as muscle tension, headaches, insomnia, and immune dysfunction.

Conversely, some people experience parasympathetic shutdown (dorsal vagal response), characterized by numbness, disconnection, or depression. Both states represent nervous system dysregulation, often fueled by unprocessed emotional pain.

Why Forgiveness is a Nervous System Process, Not Just a Decision

Forgiveness is often framed as a purely cognitive choice: “I decide not to be angry anymore.” But as Bessel van der Kolk emphasizes in The Body Keeps the Score, trauma and pain are stored in the body’s implicit memory, not just in conscious thought. This means that forgiveness requires more than intellectual agreement—it requires nervous system recalibration.

Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, points out that trauma is a physiological response that must be discharged through body awareness and movement. Forgiveness, then, can be understood as a process that helps the nervous system move out of survival mode and into safety.

Deb Dana, a leading Polyvagal practitioner, describes forgiveness as a pathway to shift from a state of threat to social engagement by activating the ventral vagal complex. This state fosters connection, empathy, and healing.

The Biology of Letting Go: What Happens When You Forgive

When you forgive, your nervous system undergoes several biological shifts:

  • Reduction in Stress Hormones: Lower cortisol and adrenaline reduce chronic inflammation and physical tension.
  • Activation of the Ventral Vagal Pathway: This promotes feelings of safety, calm, and social connectedness.
  • Release of Oxytocin: Known as the “bonding hormone,” oxytocin supports empathy and trust.
  • Improved Heart Rate Variability (HRV): Higher HRV is linked to better emotional regulation and resilience.

These changes are not just feel-good outcomes—they improve your immune function, digestion, sleep quality, and overall health.

Scientific Evidence

Research backs this up. A 2005 study published in the Journal of Behavioral Medicine found that participants who practiced forgiveness showed significantly lower blood pressure and heart rate during stress-inducing tasks. Another study in Psychosomatic Medicine (2012) linked forgiveness interventions to reduced markers of inflammation.

Forgiveness and Trauma Recovery: The Somatic Connection

For those who have experienced trauma, forgiveness can be especially challenging—and especially healing. Trauma expert Pat Ogden highlights that trauma disrupts the integration between the body and mind, often leading to fragmented memories and somatic dysregulation. Forgiveness, when approached somatically, helps to re-integrate these parts.

Gabor Maté, known for his work on trauma and addiction, emphasizes that forgiveness is not about excusing harm but about freeing oneself from the biological burden of chronic stress and resentment. This realignment allows the nervous system to settle into safety.

Practicing forgiveness through somatic techniques—such as body scanning, breath work, and mindful movement—supports the release of trapped energy and emotional charge. This is why many trauma-informed therapists incorporate somatic healing modalities alongside cognitive work.

Practical Steps: How to Use Somatic Forgiveness in Your Healing

Forgiveness is not linear or forced; it’s a process that unfolds naturally as your nervous system feels safe enough to release. Here are some evidence-backed strategies to support somatic forgiveness:

  • Body Awareness: Start by noticing physical sensations associated with anger or hurt. Where do you feel tension? How does your breath change?
  • Safe Grounding: Use grounding techniques to orient yourself in the present moment, such as feeling your feet on the floor or hands on your lap.
  • Gentle Breath Work: Slow, deep breathing activates the parasympathetic system and calms the nervous system.
  • Movement & Release: Engage in subtle movements or shaking to help discharge stored tension, as Peter Levine recommends.
  • Compassionate Reflection: Use internal dialogue or journaling to express your feelings honestly without judgment.
  • Visualization: Imagine a safe space or visualize the person you wish to forgive, noticing your bodily responses.
  • Professional Support: When possible, work with trauma-informed therapists who incorporate somatic modalities.

Common Misconceptions About Forgiveness and the Nervous System

There are several myths about forgiveness that can keep people stuck:

  • "Forgiveness means forgetting or excusing harm." Forgiveness is about releasing your own nervous system from chronic activation, not condoning bad behavior.
  • "You have to forgive quickly to heal." The nervous system regulates at its own pace; forcing forgiveness can backfire.
  • "Forgiveness is just a mental decision." It requires somatic engagement and nervous system regulation to truly take effect.
  • "If I forgive, I’m weak." Forgiveness is a courageous act of self-healing and nervous system restoration.

Conclusion: Forgiveness as a Pathway to Nervous System Freedom

Forgiveness is a profound somatic process that impacts your biology in measurable ways. By releasing resentment, anger, and bitterness, you reduce chronic stress and help your nervous system shift into a state of safety and connection. This is not about denying your pain or forgetting what happened; it’s about reclaiming your body and nervous system from the grip of unresolved trauma.

As Dan Siegel says, "Forgiveness is a gift you give yourself, a way to create coherence in your internal world." Bringing forgiveness into your somatic healing practice offers a scientifically grounded, compassionate path toward nervous system regulation and lasting well-being.

“Forgiveness is not about forgetting or excusing harm; it’s about freeing your nervous system from the burden of chronic activation.” — Deb Dana

Recommended Reading and Resources

  • The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
  • Waking the Tiger by Peter Levine
  • Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana
  • When the Body Says No by Gabor Maté

faqs.q: How does forgiveness affect my stress levels?
faqs.a: Forgiveness lowers stress hormones like cortisol and adrenaline, helping your nervous system move out of fight-or-flight mode and promoting relaxation.

faqs.q: Can forgiveness help with trauma recovery?
faqs.a: Yes, when combined with somatic healing practices, forgiveness helps regulate the nervous system, releasing trapped emotional energy and supporting integration.

faqs.q: Is forgiveness the same as forgetting or excusing harm?
faqs.a: No. Forgiveness means releasing your own nervous system from chronic stress; it doesn’t require forgetting or condoning the hurtful actions.

faqs.q: How can I practice forgiveness if I still feel angry or hurt?
faqs.a: Start with somatic awareness—notice your body’s sensations, use grounding and breathing techniques, and allow the process to unfold at your own pace without pressure.

affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that explains how trauma is stored in the body and offers insights into somatic healing and nervous system regulation.

affiliateProducts.description: This book introduces Somatic Experiencing, a body-based approach to releasing trauma and restoring nervous system balance.

affiliateProducts.title: Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana
affiliateProducts.description: An accessible guide that teaches how to apply Polyvagal Theory for nervous system regulation and healing through somatic work.
